topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a stone, heavy and cold. My stomach clenched, a familiar, tight knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, my hands trembling slightly as I scanned the text. The headline was mine—the piece I had poured weeks into, the one that felt like the culmination of everything. But the byline beneath it read a stranger’s name. A name I didn't recognize, a name belonging to someone who clearly had a knack for the spotlight. A slow, dull ache spread through my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I reread the attribution, the ink blurring slightly under my gaze. All the energy I’d invested, the late nights fueled by lukewarm coffee and sheer stubbornness, felt instantly hollowed out, replaced by a vast, echoing emptiness. I stared at the screen, the room tilting slightly, and for a long moment, nothing moved but the frantic drumming in my ears.

[story 2]

I sat at my kitchen table, the glow of the laptop screen illuminating the tired shadows under my eyes. When the notification popped up, I didn't jump; I just leaned back in my chair, the cheap wood groaning under my weight. My fingers hovered over the 'reply' button, then froze. The article was out. My words, my voice, were being used as someone else's currency. A strange numbness washed over me, a heavy blanket settling over my senses. I picked up my mug, the ceramic warm against my palms, but the heat did nothing to warm the chill that had taken root in my core. Every sentence I had crafted felt suddenly tainted, borrowed. I traced the outline of the unfamiliar name on the screen with a fingertip, a faint, bitter taste rising in my throat. It was a quiet kind of devastation, the kind that doesn't scream but settles deep, making every movement feel labored and pointless.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a monotonous soundtrack to my unraveling. I found the notification tucked between urgent client updates, and the air seemed to thin out around me. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the strain in my temples. I opened the file, and the realization hit with a sickening lurch. My contribution was there, undeniably mine, yet the authorship belonged to an imposter. A wave of profound weariness washed over me, so complete that simply lifting my head felt like an immense physical effort. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on the floor sounding shockingly loud in the silence. I didn't bother to delete the file or confront anyone; there was no strength left for that kind of fight. I just stood there, breathing shallowly, letting the weight of the situation press down until I felt anchored to the floor, unable to move, unable to look up.
